request.question: What is the stage name of the singer who qualified to perform in the final round of Lithuania in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015, along with  Monika Linkytė and Vaidas Baumila?

title: Vaidas Baumila
Serbia in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015
Mia (singer)
Poland in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015
Monika Linkytė
This Time (Monika Linkytė & Vaidas Baumila song)
Lithuania in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015
Macedonia in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015
Junior Eurovision Song Contest 2015
Australia in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015
sentences: Vaidas Jacob Baumila (born 28 March 1987 in Vilnius) is a Lithuanian singer and actor.
He represented Lithuania in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015 along with Monika Linkytė with the song "This Time".
Serbia participated in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015 with the song "Beauty Never Lies", written by Vladimir Graić and Charlie Mason.
The song was performed by Bojana Stamenov.

Serbian broadcaster Radio Television of Serbia (RTS) selected Vladimir Graić, the composer of the Eurovision Song Contest 2007 winning song "Molitva", to compose three candidate songs as potential Serbian entries for the 2015 contest in Vienna, Austria.

Graić was also tasked with selecting the performers of these three entries, opting to selected two established Serbian artists, Bojana Stamenov and Aleksa Jelić, and one undiscovered talent, Danica Krstić, to perform the three entries.

RTS held the national final "Odbrojavanje za Beč" where the song "Ceo svet je moj" performed by Bojana Stamenov emerged as the winner after topping both the votes of a jury panel and a public televote.
The entry was later translated to English as "Beauty Never Lies" with lyrics written by Charlie Mason—the lyricist of Austria's Eurovision Song Contest 2014 winning song "Rise Like a Phoenix".
Vilija Pilibaitytė (born 19 July 1983), better known by her stage name Mia, is a Lithuanian singer, songwriter, and television host.
Poland participated in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015 with the song "In the Name of Love", written by Kuba Raczyński and Monika Kuszyńska.
The song was performed by Monika Kuszyńska, who was selected by Polish broadcaster Telewizja Polska (TVP) to represent the nation at the 2015 contest in Vienna, Austria.
The selection of Kuszyńska along with her song "In the Name of Love" were presented to the public in March 2015.
In the second of the Eurovision semi-finals "In the Name of Love" placed eighth out of the 17 participating countries, securing its place among the 27 other songs in the final.
In Poland's eighteenth Eurovision appearance on 23 May, "In the Name of Love" finished in twenty-third place, receiving 10 points.
Monika Linkytė (born 3 June 1992) is Lithuanian singer who represented Lithuania in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015 along with Vaidas Baumila with the song "This Time".
She also represented Lithuania in the "New Wave 2014".
Linkytė has previously attempted to represent Lithuania in the Eurovision Song Contest in 2010, 2011, 2012, 2013, 2014 and in the Junior Eurovision Song Contest in 2007.
She was also a finalist on season two of "Lietuvos Balsas".
This Time (Monika Linkytė & Vaidas Baumila song)
Lithuania participated in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015 with the song "This Time", written by Vytautas Bikus and Monika Liubinaitė.
The song was performed by Monika Linkytė and Vaidas Baumila.

Lithuanian broadcaster Lithuanian National Radio and Television (LRT) organised the national final ""Eurovizijos" dainų konkurso nacionalinė atranka" in order to select the Lithuanian entry for the 2015 contest in Vienna, Austria.
The national selection involved artists competing independently from their songs with separate selections running concurrently to choose the winning artist and song.
"This Time" was selected as the winning song from a field of twelve competing songs.
Three artists qualified to perform the winning song in the final round of the selection: Mia, Monika Linkytė and Vaidas Baumila.

While Monika Linkytė and Vaidas Baumila entered the competition performing as solo acts, they joined forces as a duet for the final artist selection and were subsequently selected as the performers for "This Time".
Macedonia participated in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015 with the song "Autumn Leaves", written by Joacim Persson.
The song was performed by Daniel Kajmakoski.

In November 2014, Macedonian broadcaster Macedonian Radio Television (MRT) organised the "Skopje Festival 2014" in order to select the Macedonian entry for the 2015 Eurovision Song Contest in Vienna, Austria.
Daniel Kajmakoski and the song "Lisja esenski" emerged as the winner.
The song competed in Macedonia's national final in Macedonian with lyrics by Aleksandar Mitevski and Kajmakoski himself.
At the Eurovision Song Contest, however, Kajmakoski opted to perform the song in English under the title "Autumn Leaves".
In the first of the Eurovision semi-finals, Macedonia failed to qualify to the final, placing fifteenth out of the 16 participating countries with 28 points.
The Junior Eurovision Song Contest 2015 was the thirteenth annual Junior Eurovision Song Contest, and took place, for the first time, in Bulgaria.
The Bulgarian national broadcaster BNT was the host broadcaster for the event.
The final took place on 21 November 2015 and was held at the Arena Armeec in Sofia.
Poli Genova, a Bulgarian singer and former representative of Bulgaria in the Eurovision Song Contest 2011 and the Eurovision Song Contest 2016, hosted the show.
A total of seventeen countries participated, with Australia and Ireland making their debuts.
Albania and Macedonia returned after being absent since the 2012 and 2013 contests, respectively.
Croatia and Cyprus withdrew after returning in the 2014 edition , while Sweden withdrew for the first time since 2008.
Australia participated in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015 in Vienna, Austria.
Owing to its cultural and political ties to European countries, the Eurovision Song Contest has been televised in Australia by the public broadcaster SBS for over thirty years.

In honour of the contest's 60th running and in recognition of the event's popularity in Australia, it was announced on 10 February 2015 that Australia was to be invited to compete in the 2015 contest as a special guest participant.
Australians have participated in and even won Eurovision as representatives of other countries.

In 2015 for example, the Australian coloratura soprano of Armenian descent Mary-Jean O'Doherty participated in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015 contest as part of the group Genealogy representing Armenia.
But this marked the first time that the country has officially participated.

title: Lithuania in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015
Lithuania in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015
Mia (singer)

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

While Monika Linkytė and Vaidas Baumila entered the competition performing as solo acts, they joined forces as a duet for the final artist selection and were subsequently selected as the performers for "This Time".

The song was performed by Monika Kuszyńska, who was selected by Polish broadcaster Telewizja Polska (TVP) to represent the nation at the 2015 contest in Vienna, Austria. In Poland's eighteenth Eurovision appearance on 23 May, "In the Name of Love" finished in twenty-third place, receiving 10 points.

Warrior (Amber Bondin song) | "Warrior", is a song performed by Maltese singer Amber Bondin.  The song represented Malta in the second semi-final of the 2015 Eurovision Song Contest, but failed to qualify for the final.  The final version of the song was premiered along with the music video on 9 March 2015.

The Maltese broadcaster Public Broadcasting Services (PBS) organised the national final "Malta Eurovision Song Contest 2015" in order to select the Maltese entry for the 2015 contest in Vienna, Austria.  The show consisted of a semi-final round and a final where "Warrior" performed by Amber eventually emerged as the winner after scoring the maximum amount of points from both a jury panel and a public televote.  In the second of the Eurovision semi-finals, Malta failed to qualify to the final, placing eleventh out of the 17 participating countries with 43 points.

- [3] Vaidas Baumila "represented Lithuania in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015" with Monika Linkytė. (paraphrase)
- [4] Page exists for the song "This Time (Monika Linkytė & Vaidas Baumila song)". (paraphrase)

Bridge entity / missing facts:
- "Mia" is the named qualifier but the passages do not include a page for Mia or state whether "Mia" is a stage name or the performer's birth name — this is missing and potentially ambiguous (many artists use "Mia").

Prioritized next-hop retrievals to resolve ambiguity:
1. Fetch the Wikipedia page for the artist "Mia" who participated in Lithuania's 2015 national selection — possible titles to retrieve: "Mia (Lithuanian singer)", "Mia (singer)", or the specific artist page if known. Extract the opening line(s) showing stage name vs. birth name (e.g., "Mia (stage name of ...)" or "born ...").
2. Fetch the "Eurovizijos dainų konkurso nacionalinė atranka" (Lithuanian national selection) 2015 section or the "Lithuania in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015" page full article (if [1] is an excerpt) and extract the 2015 finalists listing to confirm the Mia entry and any parenthetical real name.
3. If multiple "Mia" pages exist, fetch the "Mia (disambiguation)" page and the candidate artist pages; extract biographical identifiers (birth name, country, year) to disambiguate which "Mia" qualified in Lithuania 2015.

No final answer provided because the required confirmation (the performer "Mia" page showing stage name or birth name) is not present in the supplied passages.

Måns Zelmerlöw | Måns Petter Albert Sahlén Zelmerlöw (] ; born 13 June 1986) is a Swedish pop singer and television presenter, winner in 2015 of the Eurovision Song Contest.  He took part in "Idol 2005", eventually finishing fifth, won the first season of "Let's Dance," and scored a hit with his 2007 song "Cara Mia", which was his entry in that year's Melodifestivalen.  Zelmerlöw was the host of "Allsång på Skansen" from 2011 to 2013.  He participated in Melodifestivalen in 2007, 2009 and won in 2015.  Zelmerlöw represented Sweden in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015 with the song "Heroes", winning the contest with 365 points.  Zelmerlöw and Petra Mede co-hosted the Eurovision Song Contest 2016 held in Stockholm.

Samanta Tīna | Samanta Poļakova (born 31 March 1989), better known by her stage name Samanta Tīna is a Latvian singer.  She has attempted to represent Latvia in the Eurovision Song Contest four times, in 2012, 2013, 2014, and 2016 and Lithuania in 2017, and also has taken part in the Lithuanian version of "The Voice".

Poland in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015 | Poland participated in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015 with the song "In the Name of Love", written by Kuba Raczyński and Monika Kuszyńska.  The song was performed by Monika Kuszyńska, who was selected by Polish broadcaster Telewizja Polska (TVP) to represent the nation at the 2015 contest in Vienna, Austria. In Poland's eighteenth Eurovision appearance on 23 May, "In the Name of Love" finished in twenty-third place, receiving 10 points.

Donny Montell | Donatas Montvydas (born 22 October 1987), better known by his stage name Donny Montell, is a Lithuanian singer-songwriter who represented Lithuania at the Eurovision Song Contest 2012, held in Baku, Azerbaijan.  He did so for a second time in 2016, when he represented his country in Stockholm, Sweden.

Evelina Sašenko | Evelina Sašenko (Polish: "Ewelina Saszenko" , , born 26 July 1987 in Rūdiškės) is a Lithuanian jazz singer of Polish-Ukrainian descent, who was born and lives in Lithuania.  She represented Lithuania in the Eurovision Song Contest 2011 with the song "C'est ma vie".  She is also known for participating in various television projects.  During her participation in the 2011 Eurovision, she wanted to participate under her polish name, Ewelina Saszenko.
